coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and Characterization of Dual-function H2O2-Responsive Nanoparticles for Drug Delivery to Treat Atherosclerosis
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2020 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: ZHU, Han-Yu
FANG, Chao
ZHAO, Wai-Ou
WANG, Jing-Yuan
LI, Ya-Peng
coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress is one of the major factors involved in pathogenesis of atherosclerosis. Here, a smart dual-function drug delivery system (DDS) that neutralized the plaques through its reaction with the oxidative microenvironment was fabricated. Micelles were obtained from the graft copolymer of serotonin hydrochloride, polyethylene glycol, p (itaconic acid) and aminophenylboronic-acid-linked rutin. Fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CDs) were attached to the surface of the micelles to provide dual functionality of precise treatment and diagnosis. DDS was simultaneously served as a stimuli-responsive drug delivery carrier and as a probe to detect plaques. In vitro experiments indicated that these “smart” nanoparticles could efficiently target macrophages and provide accurate imaging. At the same time, their “smart” responses to H2O2 solved the problem of sudden drug release, which could significantly improve therapeutic effect of the delivered drug.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery
Hydrogen peroxide-responsive
Dual-function nanoparticles
Atherosclerosis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 48, Issue 12, December 2020 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 ________________________ Received 2 February 2019; accepted 18 July 2020 *Corresponding author. Email: maishilou134@163.com Copyright Â© 2020, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(20)60066-4 RESEARCH PAPER Synthesis and Characterization of Dual-function H2O2-Responsive Nanoparticles for Drug Delivery to Treat Atherosclerosis ZHU Han-Yu1, FANG Chao1, ZHAO Wai-Ou2, WANG Jing-Yuan1, LI Ya-Peng1,* 1 Alan G. MacDiarmid Institute of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China 2 The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China Abstract: Oxidative stress is one of the major factors involved in pathogenesis of atherosclerosis. Here, a smart dual-function drug delivery system (DDS) that neutralized the plaques through its reaction with the oxidative microenvironment was fabricated. Micelles were obtained from the graft copolymer of serotonin hydrochloride, polyethylene glycol, p (itaconic acid) and aminophenylboronic-acid-linked rutin. Fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CDs) were attached to the surface of the micelles to provide dual functionality of precise treatment and diagnosis. DDS was simultaneously served as a stimuli-responsive drug delivery carrier and as a probe to detect plaques. In vitro experiments indicated that these â€œsmartâ€� nanoparticles could efficiently target macrophages and provide accurate imaging. At the same time, their â€œsmartâ€� responses to H2O2 solved the problem of sudden drug release, which could significantly improve therapeutic effect of the delivered drug. Key Words: Drug delivery; Hydrogen peroxide-responsive; Dual-function nanoparticles; Atherosclerosis 1 Introduction Cardiovascular diseases (CVD) are the leading causes of death and disability in a global context [1] . As one of the main factors causing CVDs, atherosclerosis (AS) has gained considerable attention in China and worldwide [2] . AS is an inflammatory disease that forms lipid-rich plaques in the arterial walls. AS has no early-stage symptoms and becomes obvious when life-threatening acute clinical conditions (such as myocardial infarction and severe inflammation) occur [3â€“5] . Therefore, prevention and treatment of AS is extremely important [6â€“8] . Currently, treatment of AS mainly involves reduction of lipid levels at a systemic level and no practical treatment for the actual site of the plague [9] . Therefore, it is an important issue that must be addressed to understand how specific pathological environment at the plaques can be used to achieve precise drug delivery. Macrophages play a major role in AS formation and rupture of unstable plaques. Their massive accumulation at the affected area help to achieve precise drug targeting [10] . In previous studies, 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) was successfully used as a target molecule for myeloperoxidase (MPO), which was over-expressed on the macrophages in the atherosclerotic region [11,12] . Compared with traditional drug-treatment methods, DDS is used in disease therapy because of their excellent size, morphology and possibility of functional modifications [13] . However, it is always a challenge to avoid drug leakage during blood circulation while rapidly releasing drugs into the target tissue. Therefore, DDS design that can release drug in response to unique stimuli is of great significance [14â€“23] . For instance, Zhao et al. [24] reported a pH-responsive nanoprobe for cancer therapy. Zhao et al. [25] reported a redox-sensitive coordination polymers as a DDS to release drugs. Kim et al. [26] developed a glucose-responsive polymer nanoreactor by ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 incorporating the glucose-responsive block copolymer with the amphiphilic block copolymer. Che et al. [27] previously reviewed advances in stimuli-sensitive polymersomes and highlighted their applications in drug delivery. Considering that AS plaques usually contain proteins, stress mutation and higher concentration of H2O2, H2O2-responsive drug delivery systems should be applied in this case [28â€“34] . Rutin is a powerful phenolic antioxidant that has various pharmacological properties, including antitumor, myocardial protecting, anti-inflammatory, and immunemodulator [5] . VoskresenskiÄ­ et al. [35] demonstrated that rutin could delay the development of hypercholesterinemia and peroxidation syndrome and tended to inhibit aortal atherosclerotic affection. Compared with organic dyes, fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) have unique advantages, such as chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Such CDs can be used in various applications, including bioimaging [36] , photocatalysis [37] , lasers [38] , light emitting diodes (LEDs) and energy conversion [39] . In this work, we synthesized excellent water-soluble CDs [40] to diagnose AS plaques based on their fluorescence effects. This work demonstrated a novel biocompatible platform for drug delivery, which could specifically target macrophage cells by nanoprobe. Drug release of this system was externally triggered because of the presence of arylboronic esters that could be degraded by H2O2 at physiological pH and temperature. Furthermore, CDs in this system were used as fluorescence probes for dual-function imaging. The physicochemical characteristics of the final drug delivery system, such as stability of the micelle, fluorescent properties, morphology, etc, were evaluated. The cytotoxicity and cell uptake to macrophage cells RAW264.7 and endothelial cells RBMEC were tested in vitro using 3-(4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and confocal laser scanning microscopy. 2 Experimental 2.1 Instruments and reagents The morphology and composition elements of the nanomaterials were investigated by ARX-500 nuclear magnetic resonance instrument (NMR, Bruker), Impat410 infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet), JEOL-2000 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Japan Electronics Co., Ltd.), UV-2450 UV-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation), Waters 410 Gel Permeation Chromatograph (GPC), and LSM710 laser confocal microscope (ZEISS, Germany). Itaconic acid (IA), Î±-methoxy-poly(ethylene glycol) (mPEG2000), aminophenylboronic acid (APBA), N-hydroxy- sulfosuccinimide (NHS), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), potassium persulfate (PPS), serotonin hydrochloride (5-HT), 1-ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd., (Shanghai, China). Rutin was purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co. Ltd. Acetone, hydrogen peroxide (30%), N,N-dimethyl amide were purchased from Beijing Chemical Plant. PBS buffer was purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. 2.2 Synthesis of poly (ethylene glycol)-p(itaconic acid) (PEG-PIA) Figure 1 shows the synthesis route of drug delivery system. PIA was synthesized by free radical polymerization using IA as a monomer and potassium persulfate as an initiator as described in our previous work [41] . Our previous results showed that 30% of PEG relative to the PIA backbone repeating unit gave PIA good performance in solvent and Fig.1 Synthesis route of CDs@5-HT-PEG-PIA-rutin (HPPR) ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 enough groups for functional modification. Thus, in this work, we first dissolved 1.0 g of PIA (Equivalent to 7.70 mmol), 0.73 g of EDC (0.38 mmol) and 0.49 g of DMAP (0.40 mmol) in 10 mL of distilled water at 30 Â°C in an oil bath for 50 min. Then 2.5 g of PEG2000 (1.25 mmol) dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water was gradually added to the solution, after which the reaction was allowed to proceed for another 18 h. The reaction mixture was then transferred into a dialysis tubing (MWCO 8000â€“14000 Da) and dialyzed against water for 72 h. 2.3 Synthesis of poly (ethylene glycol)-p(itaconic acid)- aminophenylboronic acid-rutin (PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin) The first-step to produce PEG-PIA was linked to APBA by an amide reaction. For this purpose, 0.50 g of PEG-PIA (0.685 mmol), 0.048 g of EDC (0.25 mmol) and 0.015 g of NHS (0.13 mmol) were dissolved in 12 mL of DMSO by incubation in an oil bath at 30 Â°C for 1 h. After that, 0.061 g of APBA (0.45 mmol) dissolved in 2 mL of DMSO was gradually added. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 36 h. After the reaction, unreacted impurities were removed by dialysis. After drying, the final product was pale brown solid. Approximately 0.1 g of this brown solid and 0.027 g of rutin (0.04 mmol) were dissolved in 8 and 5 mL of DMSO, respectively, after which rutin solution was gradually added to the first solution, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 36 h. After dialysis, rotary steaming and drying, a brownish-yellow powder was obtained. 2.4 Synthesis of poly (ethylene glycol)-p (itaconic acid)- aminophenylboronic acid-rutin-serotonin hydrochloride (5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin) The probe was linked by amide reaction. First, 0.1 g of PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin, 0.11 of EDC (0.05 mmol) and 0.03 g of NHS (0.026 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMSO. Then the solution containing 0.005 g of 5-HT (0.023 mmol) dissolved in 2 mL of DMSO was slowly added to this mixture, after which it was kept in an ice salt bath at room temperature for 48 h. After that, the mixture was subjected to dialysis for 4 days. The solvent was evaporated by vacuum evaporation to obtain the product. 2.5 Synthesis of CDs Approximately 3 g of citric acid and 1.5 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, and lysed in a microwave oven for 3 min to obtain a brownish yellow solid. Then 20 mL of distilled water was added and centrifuged at 7000 r/min for 10 min. Then the supernatant was vacuum evaporated to obtain CDs. 2.6 Preparation of CDs@5-HT-PEG-PIA-rutin (HPPR) and CDs@PEG-PIA-rutin (PPR) micelles HPPR micelles were prepared by dialysis. First, 3 mg of 5-HT-PEG-PIA-rutin was dissolved in 2 mL of DMSO. Then, 30 mL of deionized water was added under supersonic at 20 s per drop rate, followed by dialysis (MWCO 1000 Da) against water for 48 h. Pre-prepared CDs were added into 5-HT-PEG- PIA-rutin micelle solution, which was then dialyzed for 72 h to obtain CDs@5-HT-PEG-PIA-rutin micelles (Fig.2). PPR micelles were prepared using the same method with the same concentrations. Fig.2 Preparation of dual-function CDs@5-HT-PEG-PIA-rutin nanoparticles ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 2.7 Study of release ability of H2O2-responsive micelles First, H2O2 solutions at concentrations of 1, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25 and 0 mmol/L were prepared. Five aliquots of micelle (2 mg/mL) were placed into dialysis bags (MWCO 1000Da) for dialysis. At time interval of 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 20 and 24 h, 5 mL of dialysate was taken for measurement, and an equal amount of H2O2 solution was added at the same time. By measuring absorbance of each dialysate, the release rate was determined using rutin standard working curve obtained from absorbance data. 2.8 In vitro cytotoxicity assays Cytotoxicity of the micelle was evaluated by MTT assay. RAW264.7 and RBMEC cells were cultured in 96-well plates (100 ÂµL of cell suspension per well) for 24 h at 37 Â°C, followed by exposure to micelle solution with a series of concentrations for another 24 and 48 h. Cytotoxicities of these formulations were determined using MTT assay by adding 10 ÂµL of MTT solution into each formulation. Absorbance at 480 nm was measured after 5 h and cell survival rate was calculated. Five parallel experiments were performed for each group. 2.9 In vitro cell uptake assay Cell confocal laser microscopy imaging experiments were carried out by Shanghai Ruisai Biotechnology Co. with cultured cells and cells during testing. The nucleus was stained with DAPI and the cytoplasm was stained with F-actin. The cell suspension was inoculated on a small circular microscope (50,000 cells/well) sterilized in a 24-well culture plate, and cultured in an incubator at 37 Â°C with 5% CO2. After the cell density reached 60%â€“70%, the cells were exposed to PPR and HPPR solutions for 4 h at 37 Â°C, after which confocal laser microscopy observation was performed. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Synthesis and characterization of nanoparticles 1 H-NMR spectra and FT-IR were used to characterize the structures of final and intermediate products. 1 H-NMR spectrum of 5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin is shown in Fig.3. Peak corresponding to PEG could be seen at 3.52 ppm. Methylene on the PIA main link and its side chain exhibited the peaks located at 3.33 and 3.23 ppm, respectively. Characteristic peak of the benzene ring of APBA was at 5.98 ppm. Peak at 4.57 ppm corresponded to the amide bonds formed by the reaction of APBA and PIA, which indicated successful modification. Peaks at 1.24 and 2.64 ppm corresponded to the methyl groups of rutin and characteristic peak of 5-HT methylene, respectively. Thus, 1 H-NMR peaks proved that 5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin polymer was successfully synthesized. FT-IR spectra of PIA, PEG-PIA-APBA, PEG-PIA-APBA- rutin and 5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin are shown in Fig.4. Characteristic peaks of PEG and APBA were observed in FTIR spectrum of PEG-PIA-APBA at 2887, 1110 and 698 cm â€’1 . Thus, PEG and APBA were successfully conjugated to PIA. Peaks at 613, 511 and 792 cm â€’1 were assigned to 5-HT and rutin, confirming successful conjugation of 5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin. Fig.3 1 H-NMR spectrum of 5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 Fig.4 FT-IR spectra of PIA (a), PEG-PIA-APBA (b), PEG-PIA- APBA-rutin (c) and 5-HT-PEG-PIA-APBA-rutin (d) 3.2 Characterization of H2O2-responsive polymeric micelles Synthesized amphiphilic HPPR polymer was self-assembled into micelles in water and the size and morphology of these micelles were analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and TEM. As shown in Fig.5A, the micelles were uniformly spherical with average diameters of about 151.6 nm and the polydispersity coefficient was equal to 0.315. These results agreed with the nanoparticle size requirements applied to a human body [42] . TEM characterization results also demonstrated that the assembled micelles were uniformly round in shape (Fig.5B) with diameters only smaller (about 70 nm) to those obtained by DLS method, probably because hydrophilic fragments of these micelles shrank in dry environment during TEM analysis. 3.3 Analysis of drug release ability of HPPR micelles Absorption curves of different concentrations of rutin solution and its standard working curve are shown in Fig.6A and Fig.6B, respectively. Rutin release depends on the degradation of boronate ester bonds by H2O2. Figure 6C shows that the amount of released rutin reaches 62%, 53%, 40% and 32% after 24 h of incubation with H2O2 at different concentrations of 1.0, 0.75, 0.50 and 0.25 mmol/L, and only 5% release was obtained in the absence of H2O2. Result shown in Fig.6C and Fig.6D indicates that rutin release is H2O2-responsive and dependent on H2O2 concentration. 3.4 Fluorescence properties of fluorescent CDs Three dimensional fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared CDs were collected in 440â€“520 nm excitation wavelength range and 480â€“680 nm emission wavelength range (Fig.7A), aiming to obtain the best excitation wavelength. To achieve good imaging results, 3D spectra were converted into 2D and the results are shown in Fig.7B. Eventually 480 nm was selected as excitation wavelength because of the highest fluorescence intensity and the ability of imaging. 3.5 In vitro cytotoxicity test Cytotoxicity of different concentrations of micelles to mouse macrophage RAW264.7 cells and microvascular endothelial RBMEC cells were evaluated by MTT assay, as shown in Fig.8A and Fig.8B. Series of HPPR micelles at concentrations of below 150 Âµg/mL were incubated with the cells. Cell viability was evaluated in 24 and 48 h after initial culture. The result showed that the numbers of RAW264.7 cells decreased slightly after 48 h of incubation, and only 80% of cells survived. Figure 8B shows how HPPR micelles affect viability of microvascular endothelial cells. None of the test samples showed significant cytotoxicity against these cells after nanoparticle micelles were added. Thus, the synthesized HPPR micelles exhibited lower biological toxicity and good biological compatibility. Therefore, they could be used in physiological environment and have a potential as carriers for the disease treatment. 3.6 In vitro cellular uptake Uptake behavior of RAW264.7 and RBMEC cells was observed using confocal laser microscopy to verify targeting of micelles. Figures 9A and 9B show the uptake of nanoparticles by macrophages and microvascular endothelial cells, respectively. Fig.5 (A) Distribution of micelle by DLS; (B)TEM image of micelles ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 Fig.6 (A) UV-vis absorption spectra of rutin; (B) Standard working curve of rutin; (C) Drug release at different concentration of H2O2; (D) Drug release curves of HPPR in H2O2 Fig.7 3D spectra of CDs (A) and fluorescence intensity of CDs at different excitation wavelengths (B) Fig.8 Cytotoxicity evaluation of HPPR in macrophages RAW264.7 (A) and in endothelial cells RBMEC (B) ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 Fig.9 Confocal laser scanning microscope images of blank, PPR and HPPR in RAW264.7 macrophages (A) and endothelial cells RBMEC (B) in mice Green fluorescence indicates synthesized nanoparticles, red fluorescence corresponds to cytoplasm and blue fluorescence shows nuclear structures. Figure 9A presents RAW264.7 cells as targets. In the directional experiment, non-fluorescent blank control group did not contain nanoparticles. Experimental groups containing PPR and HPPR nanoparticles were compared with this blank group. After 4 h of incubation, cell groups modified with the HPPR probe demonstrated greater accumulation in cells with higher fluorescence intensity. Figure 9B shows the results of RBMEC microvascular endothelial cell control group. No fluorescence was observed in this control group. The blank control group was not added with nanoparticles. No significant difference between fluorescence intensities of PPR and HPPR experimental ZHU Han-Yu et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(12): e20149â€“e20157 groups was observed. Comparing with RBMEC, RAW264.7 cells demonstrated significantly higher cellular uptake. Results of the macrophage experimental group showed that nanoparticles with molecular probe 5-HT accumulated in the macrophages, which confirmed that 5-HT was specifically targeted by macrophages. Thus, the fabricated micelles demonstrated ability to specifically target macrophages as well as fluorescence imaging capabilities for diagnosis. 4 Conclusions We successfully developed nanoparticles with dual- functionality, which could be used to detect plaques by probe, realize targeted delivery of AS therapeutic agents and achieve drug-release by controlling their responses to H2O2. Targeted delivery and drug release were confirmed by in vitro experiments and MTT assays. â€œSmartâ€� responses of these nanoparticles to H2O2 improved drug utilization and therapeutic effect. Thus, HPPR could provide more efficient and safer DDS. The prepared HPPR DDS opened a new avenue for drug delivery research and development for practical applications. References [1] ComptÃ© N, Bailly B, De Breucker S, Goriely S, Pepersack T. Exp. Gerontol., 2015, 61(2): 105â€“112 [2] Tarkin J M, Joshi F R, Rudd J H. Nat. Rev. item-info.ce:copyright: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

head.ce:title: Synthesis and Characterization of Dual-function H2O2-Responsive Nanoparticles for Drug Delivery to Treat Atherosclerosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Alan G. MacDiarmid Institute of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Alan G. MacDiarmid Institute of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Alan G. MacDiarmid Institute of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress is one of the major factors involved in pathogenesis of atherosclerosis. Here, a smart dual-function drug delivery system (DDS) that neutralized the plaques through its reaction with the oxidative microenvironment was fabricated. Micelles were obtained from the graft copolymer of serotonin hydrochloride, polyethylene glycol, p (itaconic acid) and aminophenylboronic-acid-linked rutin. Fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CDs) were attached to the surface of the micelles to provide dual functionality of precise treatment and diagnosis. DDS was simultaneously served as a stimuli-responsive drug delivery carrier and as a probe to detect plaques. In vitro experiments indicated that these “smart” nanoparticles could efficiently target macrophages and provide accurate imaging. At the same time, their “smart” responses to H2O2 solved the problem of sudden drug release, which could significantly improve therapeutic effect of the delivered drug.

A “smart” response nanoparticle was successfully developed by modifying the probe, carbon fluorescence quantum dots and H2O2-responsive structure, which could respectively detect plaques by probe, realize targeted delivery of AS therapeutic agents and achieve drug-release by controlling their responses to H2O2.
